## [00:00 - 03:01]

In this lesson, I'm going to walk through the behind the scenes of July and how we started from one concept to where we are now. We had this idea of creating the perfect carry-on for customers. We basically found a local design agency called One Design Office and had a chat and we knew that these guys are the best people to realize the design for us and we just started working with them. In about one month, two months time, when we finished the design, we brought the design all the way to the manufacturing partner that I already knew through my network and let them begin the process of product development. It took them about two months time before they finalized the development and we got the target price that we wanted and that enabled us to sell the right price that we want to sell at. We were able to sell a very, very premium product at a 295 price point. At the end of October, we placed the first order. We officially launched the business in February at the beginning because the restriction of capital, we actually didn't lease a warehouse. We started using a 3PL because we were able to get very, very good rate. We managed to convince the 3PL supplier to charge us based on CBM rather than pallets We got pretty good rates and also for the delivery. We got very good rate from Australia Post and we just started shipping from February onwards. It was mid-2018 when everything began. By January 2019, we shipped out our first container and by February 2019, we received the first container. We had a lot of manufacturing challenges in the development process because we want to put full-analyzed aluminum bumpers on top of the potter carbonate shell. It's actually very, very difficult to do, something that we completely didn't realize. It's difficult to match the color. It's difficult to put the aluminum rivet to attach the potter carbonate shell. It's just very, very difficult. We workshop together with the manufacturer and we bring all the designer together to solve this manufacturing challenge.

## [03:01 - 06:05]

That's why in the market place, you don't see a lot of suitcases with two different materials, potter carbonate with aluminum. The only other case on the market with two hybrid materials is RIMO or Karyon, which they retail for $1,200. There are many, many little things like this throughout the product development process that we were working with the manufacturer. This is one of the advice that I want to give to anybody. The idea is that there will be time that factories will tell you, this is impossible or this is too hard. I think don't give up. Go to the factory, workshop with them and talk through and see if you have a better solution for them. Sometimes you can just resolve the issue by resolving the challenges by talking to them and helping them out. This is one of the best lessons that we learned in July. The second difficulty that we didn't realize at the beginning was that we didn't know that the curvature of the shell was actually determined by the wheel housing and handle housing on top, wheel housing at the bottom. Because of this, in order to achieve the X-shell design that our designer came out with, we actually had to retool the factory, the wheel factory, retool the telescopic handle factory to fit on our case. A lot of cases on the market look very, very similar because no one would spend the money to retool the factory for those components because that costs a lot of money. The product costs close to $1.25 million to develop. That was another thing. It actually costs a lot of money to retool each one of the components. In the end, we did it. I'm just so happy that we did it. There were 10 to 20 different manufacturing challenges that we had during that process. If we didn't resolve those challenges together with the factory, we wouldn't even have a business today. In the supply chain process, it's very much similar to what I said in the different lessons. You need to work first. You need to work out whether you want to do warehouse or 3PL. At the beginning of the business, we didn't have a lot of capital and we didn't want to sign any leases. We didn't do any warehousing ourselves. Only recently, we moved everything in-house. We were using 3PL to help us warehouse and fulfill orders. This is on the warehousing side. On the delivery side, we basically work with all the different carriers. We signed a deal with DHR. We signed a deal with UPS. We also signed a deal with Australia Post.

## [06:05 - 07:54]

They basically used their services to fulfill the orders. We don't have a lot of challenges. At the beginning, it was all manual. But then we discovered that we could actually use Shippet to fulfill orders. That made our life a lot easier. The other big thing that really helped with July's success was the support from our manufacturers. We were able to get three months of payment term with all our major suppliers from very, very early on. Well, this can't be applied to a lot of you, but I really encourage you to do what I did. I spent enough time with my supplier and built a personal relationship with them. They trust me. I trust them. A lot of the time, because of mutual trust, all the different requests can be met. I really encourage people to go to the factory more often and build a personal relationship with your manufacturer. We were able to get the support from our manufacturers. We didn't have to pay for container for three months. This really helped out with our growth. I think this all comes down to how much effort you want to put into building your relationship with the manufacturer. I invest a lot of my time with the manufacturer. I let them know what my difficulties are. They trust me. We made the deal. You maybe can do the same. There's no action items for this lesson. I hope you enjoyed these lessons and found value. Good luck scaling your store.
